---
title: "How mizerMR uses mizer's extension mechanism"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: yes
    fig_width: 5
    fig_height: 5
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{How mizerMR uses mizer's extension mechanism}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
library(mizerMR)
```

# Overview

This vignette is aimed at developers who want to understand how mizerMR is
built on top of mizer's extension machinery, and how a future refactor could
make the package cleaner by also adopting S3 subclassing. It assumes you have
read `vignette("extensions", package = "mizer")`.

mizer offers five main routes for extending a model. mizerMR currently uses
three of them — `setComponent()`, `setRateFunction()`, and `other_params()` —
and does *not* yet use S3 subclassing. The second half of this vignette argues
that adopting S3 subclassing would remove a class of fragility from the current
code.

# How mizerMR currently works

## The resource state lives in a `setComponent()` component

The multiple resources are stored as a single dynamical component named `"MR"`.
The component state is an array of dimensions `(resource × size)` holding the
number density of each resource at each size on mizer's full size grid.
`setMultipleResources()` registers this component:

```r
params <- setComponent(
    params      = params,
    component   = "MR",
    initial_value = initial_resource,   # array (resource × size)
    dynamics_fun  = "mizerMR_dynamics",
    component_params = list(
        rate        = resource_rate,        # array (resource × size)
        capacity    = resource_capacity,    # array (resource × size)
        interaction = resource_interaction  # matrix (species × resource)
    )
)
```

During a call to `project()`, mizer automatically calls `mizerMR_dynamics()` at
each time step. That function iterates over resources and applies semi-chemostat
dynamics to each row of the state array, using the per-resource mortality
computed earlier in the rate pipeline.

The component state at every saved time step ends up in `sim@n_other[, "MR"]`
and is exposed to users via `NResource(sim)` and `finalNResource(sim)`.

## Two rate functions replace the defaults

The built-in mizer encounter and resource-mortality rate functions work with a
single `n_pp` vector. mizerMR replaces both with versions that understand the
multi-resource array.

### `mizerMREncounter`

Registered with:

```r
params <- setRateFunction(params, "Encounter", "mizerMREncounter")
```

Instead of convolving the predation kernel against a single `n_pp`, it first
multiplies the multi-resource state array by the species–resource interaction
matrix to form a weighted prey field, adds the fish-to-fish contribution, and
then runs the FFT-based convolution against the combined field.

```r
mizerMREncounter <- function(params, n, n_pp, n_other, ...) {
    prey <- params@other_params[["MR"]]$interaction %*% n_other[["MR"]]
    prey[, idx_sp] <- prey[, idx_sp] + params@interaction %*% n
    # ... FFT convolution against prey ...
}
```

It also manually loops over `params@other_encounter` to include any additional
encounter contributions from other registered components, replicating what
mizer's built-in `mizerEncounter()` does. This is necessary because
`setRateFunction()` replaces the entire encounter calculation, so the standard
looping code no longer runs.

### `mizerMRResourceMort`

Registered with:

```r
params <- setRateFunction(params, "ResourceMort", "mizerMRResourceMort")
```

mizer's default `ResourceMort` returns a single vector (one value per size bin)
representing mortality on `n_pp`. The replacement returns a matrix
`(resource × size)` by multiplying the predation rate matrix by the
species–resource interaction:

```r
mizerMRResourceMort <- function(params, n, n_pp, n_other, t, pred_rate, ...) {
    t(params@other_params[["MR"]]$interaction) %*% pred_rate
}
```

This matrix is later passed row-by-row to each per-resource dynamics function
inside `mizerMR_dynamics()`.

## The built-in single resource is silenced

Because all resource dynamics are now handled by the "MR" component, the
built-in mizer resource must not interfere. `setMultipleResources()` does two
things to achieve this:

```r
mizer::initialNResource(params) <- 0         # abundance set to zero
resource_dynamics(params) <- "resource_constant"  # kept at zero forever
```

## Resource parameters live in two places

Resource-level parameters (kappa, lambda, r_pp, w_min, w_max) are stored as a
data frame in `other_params(params)[["MR"]]$resource_params`. The per-size
arrays derived from those parameters (carrying capacity and replenishment rate)
live inside the component's own `component_params`. This split reflects the
two-level structure recommended by mizer: `other_params()` for model-wide
state, `component_params` for data belonging to a single component.

## Result accessors and plots use manual dispatch

Functions like `NResource()`, `finalNResource()`, `plotSpectra()`, `getDiet()`,
and `plotDiet()` need to behave differently depending on whether the model has
multiple resources or not. At present this is handled with an explicit guard
at the top of each function:

```r
NResource <- function(sim) {
    if (is.null(getComponent(getParams(sim), "MR"))) {
        return(mizer::NResource(sim))   # fall back to mizer
    }
    # ... MR-aware implementation ...
}
```

This pattern works but has costs: the same `is.null(getComponent(...))` test is
repeated in every function, the functions shadow mizer's namespace without being
formal S3 methods, and there is no compile-time guarantee that the two branches
stay consistent with each other.

# How S3 subclassing could improve the package

mizer's S4 classes (`MizerParams`, `MizerSim`) are dispatched via S3 for all
user-facing functions. That means an extension package can define S4 subclasses
and write S3 methods for them, and mizer's generic machinery will dispatch to
those methods automatically — no manual `if` guards required.

Here is a sketch of what the refactor would look like.

## Define the subclasses

```r
setClass("MRMizerParams", contains = "MizerParams")
setClass("MRMizerSim",    contains = "MizerSim")
```

`setMultipleResources()` would coerce its return value to `MRMizerParams`:

```r
setMultipleResources <- function(params, ...) {
    # ... existing setup code ...
    new("MRMizerParams", params)
}
```

Because `MRMizerParams` inherits from `MizerParams`, every existing mizer
function that accepts a `MizerParams` object continues to work without any
changes.

## Dispatch plot and accessor functions automatically

Instead of the current:

```r
NResource <- function(sim) {
    if (is.null(getComponent(getParams(sim), "MR"))) {
        return(mizer::NResource(sim))
    }
    # MR-aware code ...
}
```

the package would define:

```r
NResource.MRMizerSim <- function(sim) {
    # MR-aware code only — no guard needed
    n_res <- aperm(simplify2array(NOther(sim)[, "MR"]), c(3, 1, 2))
    dimnames(n_res)[[1]] <- dimnames(NOther(sim))[[1]]
    names(dimnames(n_res))[[1]] <- names(dimnames(NOther(sim)))[[1]]
    n_res
}
```

When a user calls `NResource(sim)` and `sim` is an `MRMizerSim`, R dispatches
to `NResource.MRMizerSim`. When `sim` is a plain `MizerSim` (no multiple
resources), R dispatches to mizer's own `NResource.MizerSim`. No package-level
override is needed, and the two branches are cleanly separated.

The same pattern applies to `finalNResource`, `plotSpectra`, `getDiet`,
`plotDiet`, and `animateSpectra`.

## `project()` returns the right subclass

One extra step is needed: `project()` returns a `MizerSim`, not an
`MRMizerSim`. The simplest fix is an S3 method for `project`:

```r
project.MRMizerParams <- function(params, ...) {
    sim <- NextMethod()          # call mizer's project()
    new("MRMizerSim", sim)       # wrap the result
}
```

This ensures that every simulation result from an `MRMizerParams` is
automatically an `MRMizerSim`, so the correct `NResource`, `plotSpectra`,
etc. methods fire without the user having to do anything special.

## Summary of benefits

| Aspect | Current approach | With S3 subclassing |
|---|---|---|
| Dispatch logic | `is.null(getComponent(...))` guard in every function | R method dispatch — no guards |
| Namespace | Functions shadow mizer's generics | Proper S3 methods alongside mizer's |
| Fallback to mizer | Explicit `mizer::foo()` call inside the guard | Automatic via `NextMethod()` or non-dispatch |
| Extensibility | Third-party code must know to call `mizerMR::foo()` | Standard R generics — works transparently |
| Correctness risk | Must keep guard condition consistent everywhere | Enforced by class membership |

The extension mechanism in mizer is designed with this kind of layering in
mind. Adopting S3 subclassing would align mizerMR more closely with that
design intent and make the codebase easier to maintain and extend.
